insight: Chrono Filter combos extremely well with tech-heavy units like Antima and Wincer, providing guaranteed blue and red units every other turn that synergize with late-game payoffs, though it becomes terrible in sets without complementary tech.
author: msven, apooche
context: Discussion of Chrono Filter's role as a combo unit and its set dependency.

insight: Forcefield + Engineer defense is only cost-efficient compared to Wall if you can use the spare blue for something valuable; otherwise the drone cost makes it inefficient despite appearing cheap.
author: awaclus, p0lari, mtanzer
context: Detailed debate on when Forcefield is worth buying over Wall in early-midgame defense.

insight: When defending against multiple attackers, buying Polywall becomes efficient if you use its full 5 HP absorb and it saves you from buying any other defense next turn, but it is bad if opponent has granular attacks or you waste other tech colors.
author: kristian1103, mqp
context: Discussion of Polywall vs Wall defense tradeoffs.

insight: The Q key automatically collects gold from all drones without secondary costs and auto-selects a working defense and offense, useful for newer players unsure of their defenses but not necessarily optimal.
author: kynarethnobaka
context: Discovery and explanation of Q key quality-of-life feature.

insight: Making Forcefields is strategically similar to clicking an Ebb Turbine and spending resources on defense; decide based on whether you would click Ebb Turbine in that situation.
author: .jrkirby
context: Analogy to help evaluate Forcefield purchases.

insight: Green tech that accumulates without spending outlets (e.g., unused Forcefields) should be converted to Forcefields earlier rather than later to generate tempo by enabling blue attackers, as floating green for multiple turns is wasteful.
author: mqp
context: Analysis of when to spend green resources on Forcefields.

insight: Amporilla has many counters and won't win most sets, but completely dominates and outscales everything when it does win, making it a high-variance unit that feels oppressive ('salt unit') without being fundamentally overpowered.
author: apooche
context: Assessment of Amporilla's power level and playstyle impact.

insight: Developer Elyot noted that StarCraft 2 has too much defender's advantage due to warp gate negating positional strategy, which was a problematic design that inspired Prismata's more balanced approach.
author: elyot (Developer)
context: Design philosophy discussion comparing Prismata to StarCraft 2.

insight: In early-game Prismata play, even a single Wall that doesn't block damage for one turn can lose you the game due to inflation, whereas similar mistakes in StarCraft have less severe consequences because time is more granular and skill expression can compensate.
author: 307th, crash_overlord
context: Debate on how turn-based structure amplifies inflation importance compared to real-time RTS.

insight: Inflation is a central strategic concept in Prismata because nearly all units function as investments (including attackers that deny opponent economy), whereas in many RTS games there is a clearer separation between economy and combat units.
author: 307th, apooche, mtanzer
context: Theoretical discussion of why inflation mechanics are more important in Prismata than other strategy games.

insight: Absorber value scales with how many times it will be hit; the best absorbers are usually large health units like Rhino, Aegis, and Husk, though sometimes smaller/cheaper absorbers are better if they enable other purchases.
author: 307th, masn6811
context: Analysis of absorber unit rankings and selection criteria.

insight: Big Red units (Amporilla, Vai Mauronax, Tatsu Nullifier, Shadowfang) require heavy Red tech commitment for 3-4 Red cost, leaving the player extremely vulnerable on defense, making them high-risk high-reward plays.
author: 307th
context: Blog post analysis of large Red unit costs and tradeoffs.

insight: If your opponent can defend your attack while still building more attack, this indicates a game loss situation due to economic advantage, not a defensive decision problem.
author: apooche
context: Strategic interpretation of defensive scenarios and what they signal about game state.

insight: Chrono Filter's every-other-turn constraint creates an inherent over-teching or under-teching problem, as your economy won't align perfectly with the alternating unit availability, though early-game adoption partially mitigates this.
author: mrguy888, kynarethnobaka
context: Tactical discussion of Chrono Filter's inherent rhythm problem.
